They designed and built the Saturn V rocket — the most powerful ever built — and the ingenious Apollo spacecraft that would carry three astronauts a quarter of a million miles to the Moon.

On 20 July 1969, Apollo 11 landed on the Moon's surface. Astronaut Neil Armstrong stepped carefully down a ladder and placed his boot on the grey lunar dust. 'One small step for man, one giant leap for mankind,' he said. Around 600 million people — roughly one fifth of the world's population — watched the spectacular moment on live television.

The space age inspired generations of courageous scientists, engineers, and dreamers. The technologies developed for space exploration — satellite navigation, weather forecasting, solar panels, and much more — transformed everyday life on Earth. The legacy of those triumphant pioneers who first reached for the stars continues today, as new spacecraft head toward Mars and beyond.

image.caption: Buzz Aldrin on the Moon during Apollo 11, July 1969 — one of the most iconic photographs ever taken

articles.title: The Digital Revolution
articles.blurb: In a few short decades, computers shrank from room-sized machines to pocket-sized devices — and the internet connected the whole world.
paragraphs: In the 1940s, the first electronic computers were enormous machines that filled entire rooms, consumed huge amounts of electricity, and could only be operated by specialist engineers. They were expensive, unreliable, and incomprehensible to ordinary people. Within 50 years, a more powerful computer would fit in your pocket — and most of the world's population would own one.

The key was the transistor, invented in 1947, and then the silicon chip — a tiny wafer of material that could hold millions of transistors. These ingenious inventions made computers smaller, faster, and cheaper with every passing year. In 1971, the first microprocessor was created. In 1984, Apple released the Macintosh — the first computer that ordinary people could actually use with ease.

In 1991, a British scientist named Tim Berners-Lee made available to the world his invention: the World Wide Web. Previously, the internet had been used mainly by universities and governments. The Web made it accessible to everyone. Within a few years, the progressive spread of digital technology began to stimulate every corner of society and commerce.

The drastic impact of the digital revolution on daily life is difficult to overstate. Shopping, banking, entertainment, communication, education, medicine — every aspect of modern life has been transformed. Social media platforms amplify voices and connect people across continents in a vast, bustling global network of information. Streaming services carry entire film and music libraries in your pocket.

The collective human ingenuity that built the digital world has already changed what it means to be alive in the 21st century. There was a triumphant joy in each breakthrough — from the first email to the first website to the first smartphone. The legacy of the digital revolution is still being written, as artificial intelligence begins its own drastic transformation of human life.

image.caption: Frances Spence and Jean Bartik operating the ENIAC — one of the world's first electronic computers, in 1947
image.credit: US Army / Wikimedia Commons / Public Domain

articles.title: The Challenge of Climate Change
articles.blurb: As humans burn fossil fuels and warm the planet, the greatest challenge of our time is deciding how to protect the world we've inherited.
paragraphs: Since the Industrial Revolution began, humans have been burning coal, oil, and gas at an ever-increasing rate. These fuels release carbon dioxide — a greenhouse gas — into the atmosphere. The atmosphere traps the Sun's heat, and as carbon dioxide levels rise, the planet warms. This is climate change, and it is the defining challenge of our time.

The evidence is drastic and undeniable. Global temperatures have risen by more than 1 degree Celsius since the pre-industrial era. The Arctic ice sheet is shrinking. Sea levels are rising. Extreme weather events — hurricanes, wildfires, droughts, and turbulent floods — are becoming more frequent. Coral reefs, which support vast ecosystems, are bleaching and dying.

The Earth's climate system is fragile in ways we are only beginning to understand. Scientists warn that if warming exceeds 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, some of the consequences could be irreversible. Ice sheets in Greenland and Antarctica could melt, raising sea levels enough to flood coastal cities. The calamity, if unchecked, could be catastrophic.

The persistent challenge of climate change requires a collective response of a kind humanity has rarely managed. Governments, businesses, and individuals must all play a part. Renewable energy — solar and wind power — is growing fast, and many determined countries have committed to reaching net-zero carbon emissions by 2050. Young people around the world, inspired by activists like Greta Thunberg, are demanding action.

The fight against climate change is ultimately a fight for preservation — of our planet, our civilisations, and our futures. Every resilient generation in this history faced its own great challenge: ice ages, plagues, wars, and injustice. They found a way through. The legacy of all who came before is in your hands now. The question is: what kind of world will you choose to build?

image.caption: The Rhône Glacier in Switzerland — one of many glaciers worldwide retreating significantly due to climate change
